text: A service charge is added to the bill.
You may be charged a fee by your bank.
It is said that tourists pay more.
Excuse me, before I pay -- is service included in the bill?
Yes, a ten percent service charge is added automatically, so a tip isn't expected.
Good to know. And is there a surcharge if I pay by card?
Not from us, but you may be charged a small foreign fee by your own bank.
Then I'll pay in cash. Here's twenty -- please keep the change.
That's very kind -- thank you. Your receipt is printed here, with everything itemized.

Good afternoon, and welcome aboard. Before we begin today's tour, a quick word about money. Your ticket has already been paid, so nothing more is owed for the ride itself. However, please be aware that a small service charge is added at the two restaurants we visit, and tips are not included in that charge. At the craft market, prices are usually marked up for visitors, so a little bargaining is expected and never taken badly. If you pay by card, you may be charged a foreign transaction fee by your own bank, not by us. To keep things simple, a flat rate of twenty euros covers all entrance tickets for the day. Loose change is always useful for the small things. Enjoy the tour, and please ask if anything is unclear.

The hardest lesson I learned about money abroad was not how to count it, but how it is handled differently everywhere. On my first trip, I assumed tipping worked the same as at home. It does not. In some countries, a service charge is included and nothing extra is expected. In others, the staff are barely paid, and a tip is quietly relied on. I once left nothing, because I thought the bill had been settled in full, and I was given a very cold look. Now I always ask one simple question before I pay: is service included? If the answer is yes, I relax. If it is no, I add a little gratuity. A few coins, asked about politely, are never wasted. Money is spoken about so rarely that one honest question can save you a great deal of embarrassment.
